name: Resilience & production readiness
description: Reliability patterns from the Dennemeyer 100: circuit breakers and cascading failure, backpressure and rate limiting, SLO/SLA/error budgets, production readiness (health checks, graceful shutdown, deploy strategies, observability), idempotent retries, and the resilience toolkit.

topics.name: Circuit breaker
topics.description: Stops calling a failing dependency to prevent cascading failure; closed/open/half-open states.
topics.studyNotes:
When a downstream keeps failing, stop calling it for a while. Retrying INTO a failing dependency makes the outage worse — the breaker is what prevents that.

Three states: closed (normal, calls pass, failures counted), open (threshold exceeded — e.g. 50% of calls to the payment service failing — calls fail fast or fall back without touching the dependency), half-open (after a cooldown, a few probe calls; success closes the breaker, failure re-opens it).

The breaker exists because of cascading failure: one slow component topples the rest. The canonical chain: payment service slows → API requests wait on it → connection/thread pools fill → users retry → even more load. The defense stack against that chain: timeouts on every network call (waiting forever is worse than failing predictably — return pending or a controlled error after 3s), circuit breakers, queues, backoff, rate limits. Timeouts and breakers work as a pair: the timeout caps a single call, the breaker recognizes the pattern.

topics.name: Backpressure & bulkhead
topics.description: Limiting and isolating load so one slow dependency cannot exhaust shared resources.
topics.studyNotes:
A healthy system signals that it cannot take more work and slows intake — controlled degradation beats collapse. Signals and mechanisms: bounded queues (reject when full), HTTP 429, rate limiting, switching to async processing (202 Accepted + pending), autoscaling.

The example to give: queue age starts climbing → the API stops accepting new heavy jobs synchronously and answers 202/pending, or throttles non-critical endpoints (reporting) so the core flow stays alive.

Rate limiting vs throttling nuance: a rate limit is a fixed rule per user/API key (1000 requests/minute → 429 Too Many Requests); throttling is adaptive slowing under load. Both protect against overload, abuse, and cost explosions — and both are backpressure made visible to clients.

topics.name: SLO / SLA / SLI & error budget
topics.description: Service level indicators, objectives, agreements, and error budgets driving release pace.
topics.studyNotes:
Three letters, three different things:
- SLI — the measured indicator: latency, error rate, availability.
- SLO — your internal target on an SLI: "p95 under 500ms for 99% of the month", "99.9% successful responses".
- SLA — the contractual promise to a customer, often with compensation attached. SLAs are looser than SLOs; you want to breach your internal target long before the contract.

The error budget makes SLOs actionable: 99.9% allows ~43 minutes of downtime a month — that is budget to SPEND. Budget remaining → ship faster, take deploy risk. Budget exhausted → feature rollouts yield to reliability work, alert tuning, incident follow-ups. It is the mechanism that connects a technical decision to business risk in one sentence.

Percentile literacy belongs here: averages hide the tail. Average 80ms with p99 at 3s means your slowest users suffer invisibly — which is why SLOs are set on p95/p99, not means. And alerts should fire on SLO-relevant, actionable conditions (error rate above 5% for 10 minutes), not on noise (a single CPU spike).

topics.name: Production readiness
topics.description: Health checks, graceful shutdown, correlation IDs, alerting — what a service needs before go-live.
topics.studyNotes:
What must be true before traffic hits a service.

Health checks split in two: liveness (is the process alive — restart it if not) and readiness (can it take traffic — DB connected, config loaded). During rollout the new pod is alive but NOT ready until its dependencies are up; readiness gates the traffic. This is what makes zero-downtime deploys real.

Graceful shutdown is the other half: on termination, flip readiness to false, stop accepting new requests, finish in-flight ones, close DB/Redis connections, then exit. Without it, every deploy drops requests.

Deployment strategies ranked by risk appetite: rolling update (gradual pod replacement — the K8s default), blue-green (two environments, instant switch-back), canary (new version gets 5% of traffic; error rate rises → rollback), feature flags (code deployed dark, feature switched on separately). The theme: shrink the blast radius of every release.

Observability is the third leg — logs, metrics, traces (not just logging): a correlation ID travels through every service so one customer's failure is traceable end to end (API log → payment service log → DB latency metric); structured logs (event=payment_failed, customerId, requestId, reason) make Kibana/Grafana queries possible; alerts fire on actionable conditions.

topics.name: Idempotency & Retries
topics.description: Safe retries: idempotency keys, backoff, DLQs, and at-least-once delivery.
topics.studyNotes:
The starting assumption for external APIs and queues: retries WILL happen — timeouts, crashed consumers, at-least-once brokers. So writes must be safe to repeat.

Idempotency: the same request processed twice causes ONE side effect. The client's renewal request times out and is re-sent with the same idempotency key → the backend recognizes the key and does not create a second renewal. Mechanisms: idempotency key + unique constraint, or a processed-events table keyed by eventId.

Retry discipline: transient failures (503 from a payment provider) are retried with exponential backoff — 1s, 2s, 4s — never instantly and aggressively (that is how you finish off a struggling dependency). Invalid BUSINESS data is not retried at all: it fails fast and visibly. Distinguishing the two classes is the interview point.

DLQ — dead-letter queue: where terminally failed messages land for inspection instead of blocking the queue or being lost. Alert on DLQ depth.

At-least-once delivery ties it together: brokers typically guarantee delivery by allowing duplicates. The same PaymentCompleted event arrives twice; the consumer sees eventId already processed and does not send two invoices. Exactly-once is approximated by at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers — say it exactly like that.

topics.name: The Resilience Toolkit
topics.description: Resilience vs fault tolerance, bulkheads, fallbacks, and honest degradation.
topics.studyNotes:
Resilience: the system does not collapse entirely under failure — it degrades partially or recovers fast. The notification service dying must not stop the renewal flow; the email waits in a queue and is retried later. Fault tolerance is the narrower term: surviving CONCRETE component failures — a pod dies, Kubernetes restarts it; an instance goes unhealthy, the load balancer routes around it.

Bulkhead isolation: partition resources so one failing area cannot drag down the rest — a separate worker pool for document processing, a separate connection pool for payment calls. When document processing floods, the public API keeps answering. Named after ship compartments for exactly the right reason.

Fallback: a defined degraded behaviour when a dependency fails — serve slightly stale data from cache, mark the result pending, defer the email. The honesty rule from the source: for critical business operations, NEVER fake success. Explicit pending or failed beats a lie that unwinds later; if reporting is down, the core renewal flow proceeds and the auxiliary data shows as pending.

The toolkit composes with timeouts, circuit breakers, rate limits and backpressure (their own topics) — in an interview, pick the failure scenario first, then name which tool addresses which link of the chain.

questions.title: Circuit breaker
questions.prompt: What problem does a circuit breaker solve, and how does it work?
questions.answerStructureHint: The cascading-failure problem, the three states, and what happens to callers while it is open.

expectedPoints.label: Prevents cascading failure
expectedPoints.description: Stops hammering a failing dependency so threads/connections don't pile up and take the caller down too.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the states
expectedPoints.description: Closed (normal), open (reject immediately after error threshold), half-open (probe recovery).

expectedPoints.label: Callers need a fallback
expectedPoints.description: While open, callers degrade gracefully: cached data, default value, or a clear error.

followUps.prompt: How do circuit breakers and retries interact? When does retrying make an outage worse?

questions.title: Timeouts, retries and backoff
questions.prompt: How do you configure timeouts and retries between services so they help instead of causing a retry storm?
questions.answerStructureHint: Every call needs a timeout; retry only transient errors with exponential backoff + jitter; mind the overall budget and idempotency.

expectedPoints.label: Exponential backoff with jitter
expectedPoints.description: Growing delays between retries, randomized so clients don't retry in sync.

expectedPoints.label: Retries only transient failures, safely
expectedPoints.description: Retry 5xx/timeouts a limited number of times, not validation errors; writes must be idempotent to retry.

expectedPoints.label: Timeout budgets across hops
expectedPoints.description: Downstream timeouts must fit inside upstream ones or callers give up while work continues.

followUps.prompt: And when retries are exhausted on a queue consumer — what happens to the message?

questions.title: Bulkhead and backpressure
questions.prompt: One slow downstream dependency keeps taking down your whole API. What patterns limit the blast radius?
questions.answerStructureHint: Isolate resources per dependency (bulkhead), push back on producers (backpressure), plus circuit breaking and load shedding.

expectedPoints.label: Bulkhead: isolate resource pools
expectedPoints.description: Separate connection/thread pools per dependency so one slow call can't exhaust everything.

expectedPoints.label: Backpressure: push back on load
expectedPoints.description: Bounded queues and rejecting/slowing producers instead of buffering unboundedly.

expectedPoints.label: Detects it with saturation signals
expectedPoints.description: Watches pool saturation, queue depth and latency percentiles to see it coming.

followUps.prompt: If you must shed load, how do you decide which requests to drop first?

questions.title: SLI, SLO, SLA and error budgets
questions.prompt: Explain SLI, SLO and SLA — and what an error budget is actually for.
questions.answerStructureHint: Indicator = measurement, objective = internal target, agreement = contract with consequences; error budget balances reliability vs release speed.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes SLI / SLO / SLA
expectedPoints.description: SLI is the measured signal (e.g. success rate), SLO the internal target (99.9%), SLA the external contract with penalties.

expectedPoints.label: Error budget drives decisions
expectedPoints.description: The allowed unreliability; when it burns down, you slow releases and invest in stability.

expectedPoints.label: SLIs should reflect user experience
expectedPoints.description: Measure what users feel (latency, success rate at the edge), not server CPU.

followUps.prompt: Why is targeting 100% availability usually the wrong goal?

questions.title: Production readiness checklist
questions.prompt: A new service is about to go live. What must be in place before you'd call it production-ready?
questions.answerStructureHint: Observability (metrics/logs/alerts with correlation IDs), health checks + graceful shutdown, rollback path, and failure-mode thinking.

expectedPoints.label: Observability with alerting
expectedPoints.description: Metrics, structured logs with correlation IDs, and alerts tied to user-facing symptoms.

expectedPoints.label: Health checks and graceful shutdown
expectedPoints.description: Liveness/readiness probes and draining in-flight requests on shutdown so deploys don't drop traffic.

expectedPoints.label: Safe deploy and rollback path
expectedPoints.description: A tested way to roll back fast, ideally gradual rollout (canary/blue-green).

followUps.prompt: It's 3 AM and the service is down. What did you prepare in advance to make that call short?

questions.title: Retries without double effects
questions.prompt: A client times out and re-sends a payment request; a queue redelivers an event. How do you make sure nothing happens twice?

expectedPoints.label: Idempotency mechanism
expectedPoints.description: Idempotency key / unique constraint / processed-event table — same request, one effect.

expectedPoints.label: Backoff + failure classing
expectedPoints.description: Exponential backoff for transient errors; invalid business data fails fast, no retry.

expectedPoints.label: DLQ for terminal failures
expectedPoints.description: Dead-letter queue for inspection; alert on its depth.

expectedPoints.label: At-least-once literacy
expectedPoints.description: Brokers duplicate by design; exactly-once ≈ at-least-once + idempotent consumer.

questions.title: Degrading without collapsing
questions.prompt: A non-critical dependency (notifications/reporting) starts failing under load. How do you keep the core flow alive, and what does honest degradation look like?

expectedPoints.label: Bulkhead isolation
expectedPoints.description: Separate pools/queues so the failing area cannot exhaust shared resources.

expectedPoints.label: Fallback behaviour
expectedPoints.description: Stale-from-cache / pending / deferred work — defined, not improvised.

expectedPoints.label: Never fake success
expectedPoints.description: Critical operations show explicit pending/failed, not fabricated OK.

expectedPoints.label: Resilience vs fault tolerance
expectedPoints.description: Partial degradation/recovery vs surviving specific component failures.
